account: standard unit headword
#311 SE
Rather than fully embracing a position of __________, Alurista rejects the view that his poetry provides only negative critique; rather, he aims to reconstruct a Chicano consciousness uncolored by views imposed through colonization.Rather than fully embracing a position of __________, Alurista rejects the view that his poetry provides only negative critique; rather, he aims to reconstruct a Chicano consciousness uncolored by views imposed through colonization.
答案：B, E　oppositionality; protest
rather
rather: standard unit headword
#312 TC TRIPLE
An innovative musician, accompanist extraordinaire, and protomodern hipster, Lester Young (1909-1959) remains a (i)__________ in the jazz firmament. Some of the reasons are obvious: although he dressed with conspicuous cool, he chose an artistic trajectory that seemed calculated to (ii)__________ the spotlight--his best work was in the bands of other musicians, such as Billie Holiday and Count Basie, rather than at the front of his own. Moreover, he was shy, subtle, and sensitive--traits not usually associated with great (iii)__________.An innovative musician, accompanist extraordinaire, and protomodern hipster, Lester Young (1909-1959) remains a (i)__________ in the jazz firmament. Some of the reasons are obvious: although he dressed with conspicuous cool, he chose an artistic trajectory that seemed calculated to (ii)__________ the spotlight--his best work was in the bands of other musicians, such as Billie Holiday and Count Basie, rather than at the front of his own. Moreover, he was shy, subtle, and sensitive--traits not usually associated with great (iii)__________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, D, I　lesser light; shun; celebrity
lessershunrather
lesser: standard unit headword | shun: standard unit headword | rather: standard unit headword
#313 TC DOUBLE
In a 1952 essay, Thomas Kuhn argued that Robert Boyle's corpuscular philosophy was purely mechanistic. Kuhn, however, is not (i) __________ in attributing a strict Cartesian mechanism to Boyle. In fact, this view of Boyle (ii) __________ Fontenelle, who, in his History of the Royal Academy of Sciences of 1733, identifies corpuscularism with Cartesian mechanism.In a 1952 essay, Thomas Kuhn argued that Robert Boyle's corpuscular philosophy was purely mechanistic. Kuhn, however, is not (i) __________ in attributing a strict Cartesian mechanism to Boyle. In fact, this view of Boyle (ii) __________ Fontenelle, who, in his History of the Royal Academy of Sciences of 1733, identifies corpuscularism with Cartesian mechanism.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, E　unique; can be traced to
philosophy
philosophy: standard unit headword
#314 SE
Though the autobiography generally reflects the author's distinctively benign attitude toward others' foibles, some of its observations have __________ quality, revealing an underlying disdain not evident in earlier works.Though the autobiography generally reflects the author's distinctively benign attitude toward others' foibles, some of its observations have __________ quality, revealing an underlying disdain not evident in earlier works.
答案：C, E　a derisive; a contemptuous
reflect
reflect: standard unit headword
#315 TC TRIPLE
Why Zona Gale - a Pulitzer Prize-winning writer whose novels were both a critical and popular success - has fallen so far out of the literary canon is the fascinating subject of Deborah Lindsay Williams' recent book. Williams concludes that Gale's embrace of literary (i) __________ accounts for her eventual decline in reputation. Gale sought professional affiliation with other women writers of many stripes, and Williams contends that she has therefore been (ii) __________ by a later literary paradigm that favors (iii) __________ as a marker of artistic prowess.Why Zona Gale - a Pulitzer Prize-winning writer whose novels were both a critical and popular success - has fallen so far out of the literary canon is the fascinating subject of Deborah Lindsay Williams' recent book. Williams concludes that Gale's embrace of literary (i) __________ accounts for her eventual decline in reputation. Gale sought professional affiliation with other women writers of many stripes, and Williams contends that she has therefore been (ii) __________ by a later literary paradigm that favors (iii) __________ as a marker of artistic prowess.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, I　sisterhood; marginalized; individual exceptionalism
account
account: standard unit headword
#316 TC DOUBLE
Because of her founding of the Pastel Society of America and numerous other activities on behalf of pastel painting, Flora B. Giffuni is recognized as a (i) __________ of the medium. Her efforts (ii) __________ her hope that the art will reach a higher level of recognition.Because of her founding of the Pastel Society of America and numerous other activities on behalf of pastel painting, Flora B. Giffuni is recognized as a (i) __________ of the medium. Her efforts (ii) __________ her hope that the art will reach a higher level of recognition.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, D　mainstay; reflect
reflect
reflect: standard unit headword
#317 SE
In spite of its outwardly __________ demeanor, Guatemala City was a compelling place to be in 1954.In spite of its outwardly __________ demeanor, Guatemala City was a compelling place to be in 1954.
答案：E, F　workaday; quotidian
compelling
compelling: standard unit headword
#318 TC TRIPLE
For any art lover who has sat through a high-stakes auction, where artworks often are applauded for their (i) __________, there is something undeniably (ii) __________ about encouraging more people to see (iii) __________, rather than dollar signs, when they look at paintings on museum walls.For any art lover who has sat through a high-stakes auction, where artworks often are applauded for their (i) __________, there is something undeniably (ii) __________ about encouraging more people to see (iii) __________, rather than dollar signs, when they look at paintings on museum walls.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, G　record-breaking prices; refreshing; creativity
rather
rather: standard unit headword
#319 TC TRIPLE
If telecommuting ever did reduce traffic congestion noticeably, the excess capacity on the highways would almost certainly be quickly (i) __________ by changes in current travel patterns. For example, more people might decide to drive alone instead of using public transportation. Historically, transportation and communications have been (ii) __________ to each other--both of them increasing concurrently--rather than (iii) __________ for each other.If telecommuting ever did reduce traffic congestion noticeably, the excess capacity on the highways would almost certainly be quickly (i) __________ by changes in current travel patterns. For example, more people might decide to drive alone instead of using public transportation. Historically, transportation and communications have been (ii) __________ to each other--both of them increasing concurrently--rather than (iii) __________ for each other.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, E, I　absorbed; spurs; substitutes
augmentsubstituterather
augment: standard unit headword | substitute: standard unit headword | rather: standard unit headword
#320 TC DOUBLE
A long-running philosophical debate in the field of stratigraphy-the study of sequences of sedimentary rocks-is whether the sedimentary record represents mainly (i) __________ happenings (e.g., waves and tides) or their opposite, (ii) __________ events (e.g., hurricanes and flash floods).A long-running philosophical debate in the field of stratigraphy-the study of sequences of sedimentary rocks-is whether the sedimentary record represents mainly (i) __________ happenings (e.g., waves and tides) or their opposite, (ii) __________ events (e.g., hurricanes and flash floods).
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, E　everyday; extraordinary
represent
represent: standard unit headword
#321 SE
In person, Downing is generally rather __________, seemingly incapable of smiling, let alone laughing.In person, Downing is generally rather __________, seemingly incapable of smiling, let alone laughing.
答案：C, D　dolorous; lugubrious
rather
rather: standard unit headword
#322 TC DOUBLE
The museum’s compelling new architectural exhibition looks at 11 projects that around the world that have had major (i)__________ impacts despite modest budgets. It is part of (ii)__________ in the museum’s architecture and designing department, which in the past has championed architecture’s artistic value over its real-world consequences.The museum’s compelling new architectural exhibition looks at 11 projects that around the world that have had major (i)__________ impacts despite modest budgets. It is part of (ii)__________ in the museum’s architecture and designing department, which in the past has championed architecture’s artistic value over its real-world consequences.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, F　social; a shift in philosophy

#323 TC TRIPLE
In ancient China, tin-rich ores were not as (i) __________ as copper-rich ores, and bronze--an alloy of copper and tin--was (ii) __________ material, as tin frequently had to be transported over large distances. As a result, unalloyed copper was probably used whenever (iii) __________, since the metals used for farming and domestic purposes did not need to be as strong as the metals used for hunting and fighting.In ancient China, tin-rich ores were not as (i) __________ as copper-rich ores, and bronze--an alloy of copper and tin--was (ii) __________ material, as tin frequently had to be transported over large distances. As a result, unalloyed copper was probably used whenever (iii) __________, since the metals used for farming and domestic purposes did not need to be as strong as the metals used for hunting and fighting.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, E, H　abundant; an expensive; practical
unalloyed
unalloyed: standard unit headword
#324 SE
The scholars' hypothesis challenges the scope of human psychological and behavioral universals, but does not dispense with them entirely: the team remains confident that displays of pride, for instance, are __________.The scholars' hypothesis challenges the scope of human psychological and behavioral universals, but does not dispense with them entirely: the team remains confident that displays of pride, for instance, are __________.
答案：C, F　ubiquitous; omnipresent
dispense
dispense: standard unit headword
#325 TC TRIPLE
Models of the interior evolution of Venus suggest that the planet (i) __________ periods of relative (ii) __________ and periods of instability and rapid surface overturn. In other words, Venus may "freak out" occasionally, getting rid of its internal heat in great planetwide (iii) __________ of activity, rather than in the steadier cycling of lithospheric plates that occurs on Earth.Models of the interior evolution of Venus suggest that the planet (i) __________ periods of relative (ii) __________ and periods of instability and rapid surface overturn. In other words, Venus may "freak out" occasionally, getting rid of its internal heat in great planetwide (iii) __________ of activity, rather than in the steadier cycling of lithospheric plates that occurs on Earth.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, H　oscillates between; quiescence; paroxysms
rather
rather: standard unit headword
#326 TC DOUBLE

Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, E　provocative; suggests
ratherprovocative

#327 SE
The problem of not understanding the solar wind, a high-speed stream of particles permeating space around Earth, is not merely __________: explosive solar energy traveling through it threatens astronauts and satellites orbiting Earth.The problem of not understanding the solar wind, a high-speed stream of particles permeating space around Earth, is not merely __________: explosive solar energy traveling through it threatens astronauts and satellites orbiting Earth.
答案：A, D　academic; theoretical
permeate

#328 TC TRIPLE
The author's new novel is everything that her first was not: obvious and (i) __________ rather than understated and subtly shaded. Instead of quietly revealing themselves through tiny, unexpected gestures, the characters are forced to make (ii) __________ statements that announce exactly what they are thinking and serve primarily to underscore the author's sociological points. There are occasional glimpses of the (iii) __________ and emotional wisdom so lavishly demonstrated in the author's previous novel--gifts, the reader hopes, that will be more fruitfully employed in her next.The author's new novel is everything that her first was not: obvious and (i) __________ rather than understated and subtly shaded. Instead of quietly revealing themselves through tiny, unexpected gestures, the characters are forced to make (ii) __________ statements that announce exactly what they are thinking and serve primarily to underscore the author's sociological points. There are occasional glimpses of the (iii) __________ and emotional wisdom so lavishly demonstrated in the author's previous novel--gifts, the reader hopes, that will be more fruitfully employed in her next.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, I　melodramatic; tendentious; psychological acuity

rather: standard unit headword

Fedigan argues that, in actuality, ethologists who claim to __________ anthropomorphism often end up simply substituting one set of anthropomorphic terms for another.Fedigan argues that, in actuality, ethologists who claim to __________ anthropomorphism often end up simply substituting one set of anthropomorphic terms for another.
答案：A, F　eschew; avoid
substitute

#331 TC TRIPLE
Biographies tumble off the presses in profusion these days, a (i) __________ linked, for better or worse, to the current obsession with celebrity. Some critics complain that biographers too often (ii) __________ the messy reality of a life, and it's true that lesser authors simply glorify or, more often, vilify their subjects. But good biographers are far more respectful of (iii) __________: the best see not one monolithic truth about a person but many overlapping truths.Biographies tumble off the presses in profusion these days, a (i) __________ linked, for better or worse, to the current obsession with celebrity. Some critics complain that biographers too often (ii) __________ the messy reality of a life, and it's true that lesser authors simply glorify or, more often, vilify their subjects. But good biographers are far more respectful of (iii) __________: the best see not one monolithic truth about a person but many overlapping truths.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, H　boom; impose a specious coherence on; complexity
lesser
lesser: standard unit headword
#332 TC DOUBLE
Many of the unusual behaviors attributed to crows--such as drinking coffee or presenting gifts to people who feed them--are based on (i) __________ and therefore fall into the category of (ii) __________ rather than science.Many of the unusual behaviors attributed to crows--such as drinking coffee or presenting gifts to people who feed them--are based on (i) __________ and therefore fall into the category of (ii) __________ rather than science.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, D　secondhand testimony; anecdote

#333 SE
The __________ markings on some prehistoric bone and antler fragments could be accidents or the result of doodling, but may also represent a form of communication such as tally marks or even early calendars.The __________ markings on some prehistoric bone and antler fragments could be accidents or the result of doodling, but may also represent a form of communication such as tally marks or even early calendars.
答案：B, D　enigmatic; inscrutable
represent
represent: standard unit headword
#334 TC TRIPLE
For a philosopher of the analytic tradition, Williams' approach is somewhat (i) __________. Unlike most analytic philosophers, Williams has not kept himself (ii) __________ the rest of the humanities; in fact, he wishes to address colleagues in other fields in terms that will make sense to them. In particular, he objects to the (iii) __________ character of analytic philosophy: the amount it tries to accomplish by conceptual analysis and a priori argument alone. Williams is convinced that philosophy must use history, including historical imagination, to understand and defend values of any kind.For a philosopher of the analytic tradition, Williams' approach is somewhat (i) __________. Unlike most analytic philosophers, Williams has not kept himself (ii) __________ the rest of the humanities; in fact, he wishes to address colleagues in other fields in terms that will make sense to them. In particular, he objects to the (iii) __________ character of analytic philosophy: the amount it tries to accomplish by conceptual analysis and a priori argument alone. Williams is convinced that philosophy must use history, including historical imagination, to understand and defend values of any kind.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, D, H　unorthodox; aloof from; strongly ahistorical
philosophy
philosophy: standard unit headword
#335 TC DOUBLE
Fallows argues that America's two-and-a-half centuries of rapid economic growth, rather than portending endless, similarly paced future advances, have amounted to harvesting the "low-hanging fruit" of open land, cheap energy, and industrial-era breakthroughs. Thus, such growth has been (i) __________ episode that is unlikely to be (ii) __________.Fallows argues that America's two-and-a-half centuries of rapid economic growth, rather than portending endless, similarly paced future advances, have amounted to harvesting the "low-hanging fruit" of open land, cheap energy, and industrial-era breakthroughs. Thus, such growth has been (i) __________ episode that is unlikely to be (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, D　a unique; sustained
rather
rather: standard unit headword
#336 SE
The capacity for rational thought and its manifestation in language have long been assumed to distinguish human from nonhuman animals, but apes' proven ability to learn and teach sign language to other apes suggests that such a strict human-animal divide is __________.The capacity for rational thought and its manifestation in language have long been assumed to distinguish human from nonhuman animals, but apes' proven ability to learn and teach sign language to other apes suggests that such a strict human-animal divide is __________.
答案：B, C　indefensible; untenable
untenabledivide

Flaherty understands that biological theories of artistic creation may (i) __________ people who cherish the idea that art comes from something other than the action of neurotransmitters, and she spends much time trying to shepherd us through the difficulty of accepting that the mind is actually the brain, a physical organ. She thinks we can (ii) __________ this without (iii) __________ the more exalted concepts of inspiration, the "inner voice," and so on. She, too, believes in those things.Flaherty understands that biological theories of artistic creation may (i) __________ people who cherish the idea that art comes from something other than the action of neurotransmitters, and she spends much time trying to shepherd us through the difficulty of accepting that the mind is actually the brain, a physical organ. She thinks we can (ii) __________ this without (iii) __________ the more exalted concepts of inspiration, the "inner voice," and so on. She, too, believes in those things.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, G　dismay; comprehend; discarding
cherish
cherish: standard unit headword
#338 TC DOUBLE
The grinding stones featured in some of Puerto Rico's restaurants as part of their decor evoke an idealized rustic past, but the nostalgia is (i) __________: in reality, the job of grinding corn was among the most (ii) __________ of domestic chores, often performed by the women of the house.The grinding stones featured in some of Puerto Rico's restaurants as part of their decor evoke an idealized rustic past, but the nostalgia is (i) __________: in reality, the job of grinding corn was among the most (ii) __________ of domestic chores, often performed by the women of the house.

There are forces, both external and internal, on scientists that almost require them to (iii)__________. Without money, there is no science. Researchers must constantly convince administrators who control tax dollars, investors, and individual donors that the work they are doing will make a difference.Medical research scientists’ (i)__________ claims regarding the (ii)__________ new research make the public wonder why their own doctors are not dispensing miracles. There are forces, both external and internal, on scientists that almost require them to (iii)__________. Without money, there is no science. Researchers must constantly convince administrators who control tax dollars, investors, and individual donors that the work they are doing will make a difference.

Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, H　inflated; potential benefits of; oversell

答案：B, D　averted; forestalled

#355 TC TRIPLE
For many years, Americans have had a love affair with ferryboats. Ferries are said to relieve our frayed nerves after we’ve stewed in bumper-to-bumper traffic, and conventional wisdom also says ferries (i)__________ congestion and air pollution by getting us out of our cars. Unfortunately, this (ii)__________ notion recently has (iii)__________ several West Coast mayors, who have in consequence eagerly pursued the implementation of ferry service in their cities.For many years, Americans have had a love affair with ferryboats. Ferries are said to relieve our frayed nerves after we’ve stewed in bumper-to-bumper traffic, and conventional wisdom also says ferries (i)__________ congestion and air pollution by getting us out of our cars. Unfortunately, this (ii)__________ notion recently has (iii)__________ several West Coast mayors, who have in consequence eagerly pursued the implementation of ferry service in their cities.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, E, G　reduce; misguided; captivated
coverprovocative
cover: standard unit headword | provocative: standard unit headword
#356 TC DOUBLE
For a genre that's supposed to be about the future, science fiction has certainly (i) __________ lately, (ii) __________ is what sells best, with readers spending their money on sequels to long-running series.For a genre that's supposed to be about the future, science fiction has certainly (i) __________ lately, (ii) __________ is what sells best, with readers spending their money on sequels to long-running series.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, F　been looking backward; nostalgia

答案：A, F　unassuming; modest

acerbic: standard unit headword

There is no sense trying to rehabilitate the reputation of the mosquito; nobody loves such a creature. But it’s (i)__________ to (ii)__________ all 2,600 described species of mosquito when it’s just 80 or so—3 percent—that drink human blood. Among those 2,520 relatively (iii)__________ kinds of mosquitoes, there’s even one we’d like to see in greater numbers: Taxorhynchites, the mosquito that eats other mosquitoes.There is no sense trying to rehabilitate the reputation of the mosquito; nobody loves such a creature. Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, G　unfair; malign; blameless

This (i) __________ structure inevitably emphasizes drama at the expense of (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, E　episodic; completeness

答案：B, E　exalt; valorize

This durable myth, which (i)__________ that nothing should have been known of pasta in Italy until 1295, when Marco Polo returned from the Far East, can easily be (ii)__________ by pointing out that there are Italian references to pasta that (iii)__________.One sometimes hears that Marco Polo introduced pasta to the Western world, having encountered it in China. 答案：A, E, I　requires; debunked; predate that event

His book is certainly enlightening, and although the bibliography includes technical works liable to (ii)__________ the newcomer, the work is written at a level that generally (iii)__________ between elementary and advanced.Anthony Kenny states that although his A New History of Western Philosophy is intended for undergraduates, he aimed to write it in a lighthearted manner that will give (i)__________ to those who read the history "not for curricular purposes but for their own enjoyment". Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3

Blank 3
答案：B, E, G　misrepresent; efficiency; a comprehensive historical account

By cultivating instinctive, irrational awarenesses--which might come from dreams or from accepting results derived by chance or from a receptivity to taboo--they hoped to (iii) __________ their audience's sense of everyday reality.The artists sought to (i) __________ precisely the impulses that traditional or generally accepted thinking had (ii) __________. In reality, however, these claims to a radical political agenda were often (i) __________ meant to give lurid thrillers the appearance of (ii) __________.A hallmark of certain nineteenth-century mystery novels was the reform agenda of their authors, who ostensibly sought to expose economic injustice while depicting the seamy underside of urban life. In reality, however, these claims to a radical political agenda were often (i) __________ meant to give lurid thrillers the appearance of (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, E　disingenuous; high-minded purpose

#388 TC TRIPLE
It is possible for human to go 40 or more hours without sleep and still be able to (i)__________ information acquired at the beginning of the sleepless period. Thus, when we are considering a role for sleep in human memory consolidation, we are referring to a possible role in the (ii)__________ encoding of information and optimizing of recall, not a (iii)__________ of sleep for recalling events of the prior day.It is possible for human to go 40 or more hours without sleep and still be able to (i)__________ information acquired at the beginning of the sleepless period. Thus, when we are considering a role for sleep in human memory consolidation, we are referring to a possible role in the (ii)__________ encoding of information and optimizing of recall, not a (iii)__________ of sleep for recalling events of the prior day.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, G　disgorge; longer-term; requirement

MRI scans of an elephant's brain suggest that even relative to its overall size it has a large hippocampus, the component in the mammalian brain linked to memory and an important part of its limbic system, which is involved in processing emotions.There has been (i) __________ elephant's fabled mental capacities until recently, when these behavioral observations have begun to be (ii) __________ by brain science. MRI scans of an elephant's brain suggest that even relative to its overall size it has a large hippocampus, the component in the mammalian brain linked to memory and an important part of its limbic system, which is involved in processing emotions.

Blank 2
答案：C, D　only anecdotal evidence for; buttressed

Not only was Pushkin’s personal correspondence intercepted and his movements (iii)__________, but Tsar Nicholas I’s decision to oversee Pushkin’s career obliged Pushkin to submit all his manuscripts for inspection.Even the reader acquainted with the outlines of Pushkin’s biography will be (i)__________ the (ii)__________ so vividly conveyed in Binyon’s biography. Not only was Pushkin’s personal correspondence intercepted and his movements (iii)__________, but Tsar Nicholas I’s decision to oversee Pushkin’s career obliged Pushkin to submit all his manuscripts for inspection.

Blank 2

答案：A, D　glut; surfeit

Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, G　an honorific; established; disputed

答案：C, E　polymathic; encyclopedic

Psychologists note that in making purchase decisions, consumers often unconsciously use mental shortcuts, such as familiarity with a brand name to (i) __________ routine problems efficiently. Thus, part of what companies aim for developing a brand is very specifically to (ii) __________ consumers' conscious thought.Psychologists note that in making purchase decisions, consumers often unconsciously use mental shortcuts, such as familiarity with a brand name to (i) __________ routine problems efficiently. Thus, part of what companies aim for developing a brand is very specifically to (ii) __________ consumers' conscious thought.
Blank 1
Blank 2

#397 TC TRIPLE
The writer argues that jewelry-grade diamonds, because they are both (i)__________ and (ii)__________, prove one of two conclusions: their purchasers have so much money they can spend it on goods that (iii)__________, or their purchasers are so committed to making others think they have such wealth that they are willing to go into debt to do so.The writer argues that jewelry-grade diamonds, because they are both (i)__________ and (ii)__________, prove one of two conclusions: their purchasers have so much money they can spend it on goods that (iii)__________, or their purchasers are so committed to making others think they have such wealth that they are willing to go into debt to do so.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, I　expensive; useless; serve no practical purpose
reflect
reflect: standard unit headword
#398 SE
Caricature can be revealing as well as amusing, and Ager’s novel is both: Ager’s delineation of class, ethnic, and generational struggle is exaggerated for comical effect, but it __________ nonetheless.Caricature can be revealing as well as amusing, and Ager’s novel is both: Ager’s delineation of class, ethnic, and generational struggle is exaggerated for comical effect, but it __________ nonetheless.
答案：A, E　resonates; rings true
fall short
fall short: standard unit headword

Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, D　forsaken; conventional

Unlike most other serious journals, which drain money from their owners, the Review has long been (i)__________. But the formula is not without its imperfections, which have grown more pronounced in recent years. The publication has always been erudite and (ii)__________ but not always lively and readable. (iii)__________, accompanied by a certain aversion to risk taking, has pervaded its pages for a long time.Unlike most other serious journals, which drain money from their owners, the Review has long been (i)__________. But the formula is not without its imperfections, which have grown more pronounced in recent years. The publication has always been erudite and (ii)__________ but not always lively and readable. (iii)__________, accompanied by a certain aversion to risk taking, has pervaded its pages for a long time.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, I　lucrative; authoritative; A staleness
animate
animate: standard unit headword
#401 SE
Travel writers have a special burden of forming __________ story—that is, one with a beginning, middle, and end—out of the unformed moments that make up the act of traveling.Travel writers have a special burden of forming __________ story—that is, one with a beginning, middle, and end—out of the unformed moments that make up the act of traveling.
答案：B, E　a coherent; an orderly

compelling: standard unit headword
#402 TC DOUBLE
Early in the development of ice age theories, physicists identified the precession (slow gyration) and wobbling of Earth's spin axis as the likely drivers of the ice ages, but geologists (i) __________, and even the painstaking work done in the 1920s and 1930s by Milutin Milankovitch failed to (ii) __________ of many of the day's geological experts.Early in the development of ice age theories, physicists identified the precession (slow gyration) and wobbling of Earth's spin axis as the likely drivers of the ice ages, but geologists (i) __________, and even the painstaking work done in the 1920s and 1930s by Milutin Milankovitch failed to (ii) __________ of many of the day's geological experts.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, E　demurred; assuage the qualms
anticipate
anticipate: standard unit headword
#403 TC TRIPLE
Keith Haring’s cartoonish art became even more (i)__________ than Andy Warhol’s much-reproduced soup cans when, in 1986, he opened his own store, the Pop Shop, to sell licensed Haring merchandise. The public loved souvenirs; the critics (ii)__________ what they saw as his betrayal of artistic integrity. Haring claimed that these critics misunderstood his (iii)__________ to take art beyond galleries and museums, and thus to give a wider audience the opportunity to experience art.Keith Haring’s cartoonish art became even more (i)__________ than Andy Warhol’s much-reproduced soup cans when, in 1986, he opened his own store, the Pop Shop, to sell licensed Haring merchandise. The public loved souvenirs; the critics (ii)__________ what they saw as his betrayal of artistic integrity. Haring claimed that these critics misunderstood his (iii)__________ to take art beyond galleries and museums, and thus to give a wider audience the opportunity to experience art.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, E, I　commercial; decried; sincere effort
anticipate
anticipate: standard unit headword
#404 SE
Governments are often willing to pay the direct costs of preparing for emergencies that may never happen: they make room in their budgets to prepare for __________ but unlikely events.Governments are often willing to pay the direct costs of preparing for emergencies that may never happen: they make room in their budgets to prepare for __________ but unlikely events.
答案：A, D　plausible; conceivable
anticipate
anticipate: standard unit headword
#405 TC DOUBLE
At first glance, each of the stories in the anthology seemed (i) __________; upon closer inspection of their protagonists, implied morals, and fantasy elements, however, subtle similarities among the stories emerged to (ii) __________ this initial observation.At first glance, each of the stories in the anthology seemed (i) __________; upon closer inspection of their protagonists, implied morals, and fantasy elements, however, subtle similarities among the stories emerged to (ii) __________ this initial observation.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, D　idiosyncratic; impugn
compelling
compelling: standard unit headword
#406 TC TRIPLE
The motives of many major investors in Pop Art have arguably been, too a large extent, (i)__________. These collectors demonstrate and enhance their power over the art market by establishing seemingly arbitrary works of art as priceless. This phenomenon reveals that (ii)__________ is not (iii)__________ of truth or beauty, but simply a trick of investment capital.The motives of many major investors in Pop Art have arguably been, too a large extent, (i)__________. These collectors demonstrate and enhance their power over the art market by establishing seemingly arbitrary works of art as priceless. This phenomenon reveals that (ii)__________ is not (iii)__________ of truth or beauty, but simply a trick of investment capital.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, G　self-aggrandizing; value; a product
virtuosity
virtuosity: standard unit headword
#407 SE
Citing the corruption and intrigue that pervaded politics in the city, my colleague __________ the newspaper’s trove of journalism prizes, declaring that finding great stories in the city must be effortless.Citing the corruption and intrigue that pervaded politics in the city, my colleague __________ the newspaper’s trove of journalism prizes, declaring that finding great stories in the city must be effortless.
答案：A, D　slighted; disparaged
commend
commend: standard unit headword
#408 TC DOUBLE
An architect of Koolhaas' (i) __________ might plausibly prefer designing on paper to building in concrete. Remaining within the realm of his own imagination, he need not worry about pesky clients who can dilute a project into mediocrity. But in fact what Koolhaas enjoys most is the opportunity to engage with a client. When asked if he would consider designing a house for himself, he replied that the idea bored him, that it would feel too (ii) __________. For him, the whole point of architecture is the encounter with the other.An architect of Koolhaas' (i) __________ might plausibly prefer designing on paper to building in concrete. Remaining within the realm of his own imagination, he need not worry about pesky clients who can dilute a project into mediocrity. But in fact what Koolhaas enjoys most is the opportunity to engage with a client. When asked if he would consider designing a house for himself, he replied that the idea bored him, that it would feel too (ii) __________. For him, the whole point of architecture is the encounter with the other.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, F　unfettered originality; solipsistic
unalloyed
unalloyed: standard unit headword
#409 TC TRIPLE
Human-caused disturbances, such as habitat destruction and the introduction of nonnative species, are among the leading causes of plant and animal population declines. Most populations are affected by a combination of adverse human pressures, each of which is in itself insufficient to (i)__________ a population crash. Therefore, studies of population decline that (ii)__________ individual factors and thus (iii)__________ potential interactions may lead to improper management of declining species.Human-caused disturbances, such as habitat destruction and the introduction of nonnative species, are among the leading causes of plant and animal population declines. Most populations are affected by a combination of adverse human pressures, each of which is in itself insufficient to (i)__________ a population crash. Therefore, studies of population decline that (ii)__________ individual factors and thus (iii)__________ potential interactions may lead to improper management of declining species.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, E, H　trigger; focus on; overlook
anticipate
anticipate: standard unit headword
#410 SE
Because movie studios, under pressure to generate international sales, have favored big-budget pictures with fantasy plots, the representation of everyday domestic life has largely been __________ other media, such as television and literature.Because movie studios, under pressure to generate international sales, have favored big-budget pictures with fantasy plots, the representation of everyday domestic life has largely been __________ other media, such as television and literature.
答案：A, B　left to; ceded to

substitute: standard unit headword
#411 TC DOUBLE
Literary salons and coffeehouses (i) __________ the class-based systems of social hierarchies prevalent at the time. Meritocratic appreciation of wit and brilliance, wherever it might issue, replaced (ii) __________ to established power.Literary salons and coffeehouses (i) __________ the class-based systems of social hierarchies prevalent at the time. Meritocratic appreciation of wit and brilliance, wherever it might issue, replaced (ii) __________ to established power.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, E　broke down; deference

anticipate: standard unit headword
#412 TC TRIPLE
Many researchers assume that politicians consider the preferences of the entire public, and not merely those of likely voters, when making decisions. Yet, since the desire to be reelected is central, it is plausible that politicians (i)__________ the opinions of likely voters in formulating their positions on issues. It is possible that researchers’ suppositions about policy makers’ (ii)__________ aggregate public opinion may serve to (iii)__________ the fact that only the preferences of likely voters actually matter.Many researchers assume that politicians consider the preferences of the entire public, and not merely those of likely voters, when making decisions. Yet, since the desire to be reelected is central, it is plausible that politicians (i)__________ the opinions of likely voters in formulating their positions on issues. It is possible that researchers’ suppositions about policy makers’ (ii)__________ aggregate public opinion may serve to (iii)__________ the fact that only the preferences of likely voters actually matter.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, G　attend disproportionately to; responsiveness to; mask
reflect
reflect: standard unit headword
#413 SE
Baker was struck by the amount of __________ she saw at the renowned medical facility; for all their experience, the physicians could not seem to agree on the correct diagnosis for any given patient.Baker was struck by the amount of __________ she saw at the renowned medical facility; for all their experience, the physicians could not seem to agree on the correct diagnosis for any given patient.
答案：A, B　discordance; contention

#414 TC DOUBLE

Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, F　an inventive; ineligible for

答案：B, D　deflate; soothe

Blank 2

答案：A, E, I　commercial; decried; sincere need

#417 TC DOUBLE
Scientists were jubilant to discover a remarkably well-preserved sixteenth-century vessel in the icy waters of the Baltic Sea, but they were (i) __________ by the discovery, a lack of oxygen in icy depths can (ii) __________ the usual riot of creatures that like to feast on lost wooden ships.Scientists were jubilant to discover a remarkably well-preserved sixteenth-century vessel in the icy waters of the Baltic Sea, but they were (i) __________ by the discovery, a lack of oxygen in icy depths can (ii) __________ the usual riot of creatures that like to feast on lost wooden ships.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, F　not entirely surprised; discourage

divide: standard unit headword
#418 SE
Researchers have recorded around 60 separate behaviors for worker honeybees, a number that seems to __________ the achievements of many mammals: even the versatile bottlenose dolphin only performs about twice the number a worker honeybee manages.Researchers have recorded around 60 separate behaviors for worker honeybees, a number that seems to __________ the achievements of many mammals: even the versatile bottlenose dolphin only performs about twice the number a worker honeybee manages.
答案：B, E　eclipse; outdo
reflect
reflect: standard unit headword
#419 TC TRIPLE
The essential difference between writing nonfiction and writing fiction is that the artist can (i)__________ a completed vision of the world, while the journalist never can, the real world being always (ii)__________. Art provides freedom from the bewildering complexities of constant change. Indeed, it is the very (iii)__________ of well-wrought fiction that can sometimes make it feel more real than reality.The essential difference between writing nonfiction and writing fiction is that the artist can (i)__________ a completed vision of the world, while the journalist never can, the real world being always (ii)__________. Art provides freedom from the bewildering complexities of constant change. Indeed, it is the very (iii)__________ of well-wrought fiction that can sometimes make it feel more real than reality.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, I　realize; unfinished; clarity
shun
shun: standard unit headword
#420 TC DOUBLE
Some dietary experts suggest that we should (i) __________ the traditional notion that low-fat diets are salutary, since, according to these researchers, a low-fat diet has (ii) __________ body weight or general health, and focus instead on the quality of the carbohydrates we are eating.Some dietary experts suggest that we should (i) __________ the traditional notion that low-fat diets are salutary, since, according to these researchers, a low-fat diet has (ii) __________ body weight or general health, and focus instead on the quality of the carbohydrates we are eating.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, D　jettison; no special benefit for

#421 SE
The latest publications predicting disastrous coastal erosion are unlikely to __________ knowledgeable readers because variations on the same claims have been effectively refuted in the past few years.The latest publications predicting disastrous coastal erosion are unlikely to __________ knowledgeable readers because variations on the same claims have been effectively refuted in the past few years.
答案：D, E　alarm; unsettle
baffle
baffle: standard unit headword
#422 TC TRIPLE
Within the culture as a whole, the natural sciences have been so successful that the word "scientific" is often used in (i)__________ manner: it is often assumed that to call something "scientific" is to imply that its reliability has been (ii)__________ by methods whose results cannot reasonably be (iii)__________.Within the culture as a whole, the natural sciences have been so successful that the word "scientific" is often used in (i)__________ manner: it is often assumed that to call something "scientific" is to imply that its reliability has been (ii)__________ by methods whose results cannot reasonably be (iii)__________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, I　an honorific; established; disputed

anticipate: standard unit headword
#423 TC DOUBLE
Mead was certainly (i) __________ to state the case for Arapesh nonviolence and gentleness in absolute terms, for the Mountain Arapesh had an established tradition of warfare. As a relative proposition, however, her position was more (ii) __________: compared to other groups in New Guinea, the Mountain Arapesh might have seemed unaggressive and unwarlike.Mead was certainly (i) __________ to state the case for Arapesh nonviolence and gentleness in absolute terms, for the Mountain Arapesh had an established tradition of warfare. As a relative proposition, however, her position was more (ii) __________: compared to other groups in New Guinea, the Mountain Arapesh might have seemed unaggressive and unwarlike.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, F　mistaken; defensible
prescient
prescient: standard unit headword
#424 SE
The author take issues with the ideological blinders that have distorted much migration research, especially __________ modernization theorists and others for their untested assumptions of an immobile preindustrial past.The author take issues with the ideological blinders that have distorted much migration research, especially __________ modernization theorists and others for their untested assumptions of an immobile preindustrial past.
答案：C, D　castigating; chastising
commend
commend: standard unit headword
#425 TC TRIPLE
The writer argues that jewelry-grade diamonds, because they are both (i)__________ and (ii)__________, prove one of two conclusions; their purchasers have so much money they can spend it on goods that (iii)__________, or their purchasers are so committed to making others think they have such wealth that they are willing to go into debt to do so.The writer argues that jewelry-grade diamonds, because they are both (i)__________ and (ii)__________, prove one of two conclusions; their purchasers have so much money they can spend it on goods that (iii)__________, or their purchasers are so committed to making others think they have such wealth that they are willing to go into debt to do so.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, I　expensive; useless; serve no practical purpose
reflect
reflect: standard unit headword
#426 TC DOUBLE
The political theorist argues that passing laws to (i) __________ the state in order to preclude abuse of its citizenry sounds appealing and even necessary, but doing so can (ii) __________ the state's ability to protect its citizens from both external and internal aggression. Most citizens want a compromise: a state strong enough to protect them but not so strong that it can harm them.The political theorist argues that passing laws to (i) __________ the state in order to preclude abuse of its citizenry sounds appealing and even necessary, but doing so can (ii) __________ the state's ability to protect its citizens from both external and internal aggression. Most citizens want a compromise: a state strong enough to protect them but not so strong that it can harm them.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, E　constrain; vitiate
augment
augment: standard unit headword
#427 SE
Because archaeology explores the most profound changes in human history by means of a grossly incomplete record, it has invited the sort of bold, imaginative interpretation in which speculation too easily becomes __________ evidence.Because archaeology explores the most profound changes in human history by means of a grossly incomplete record, it has invited the sort of bold, imaginative interpretation in which speculation too easily becomes __________ evidence.
答案：C, D　untethered from; divorced from

substitute: standard unit headword

Photography (ii)__________ modern arts, but when it is used as a tracing tool in order to (iii)__________ the difficulties of achieving correct proportion, the resulting art often feels static and lifeless.The (i)__________ quality of much contemporary drawing may be attributable to the use of photography as a drawing shortcut. Photography (ii)__________ modern arts, but when it is used as a tracing tool in order to (iii)__________ the difficulties of achieving correct proportion, the resulting art often feels static and lifeless.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, E, I　inert; wonderfully enriched; circumvent

In the author's view, the idea that individuals have different learning styles, such as auditory or kinesthetic, is (i) __________; it may be highly intuitive to many, but it is simply (ii) __________.In the author's view, the idea that individuals have different learning styles, such as auditory or kinesthetic, is (i) __________; it may be highly intuitive to many, but it is simply (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, D　a pernicious myth; erroneous

In establishing that the dust she had observed constitutes two percent of the mass in the quadrant, the astronomer showed that the dust's extreme visual prominence __________ its relatively minor contribution to the total mass of the region.In establishing that the dust she had observed constitutes two percent of the mass in the quadrant, the astronomer showed that the dust's extreme visual prominence __________ its relatively minor contribution to the total mass of the region.
答案：A, B　belies; masks

Unlike most other serious journals, which drain money from their owners, the Review has long been (i)__________. But the formula is not without its imperfections, which have grown more pronounced in recent years. The publication has always been erudite and (ii)__________ but not always lively and readable. (iii)__________, accompanied by a certain aversion to risk taking, has pervaded its pages for a long time.Unlike most other serious journals, which drain money from their owners, the Review has long been (i)__________. But the formula is not without its imperfections, which have grown more pronounced in recent years. The publication has always been erudite and (ii)__________ but not always lively and readable. (iii)__________, accompanied by a certain aversion to risk taking, has pervaded its pages for a long time.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, I　lucrative; authoritative; a staleness

Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, D　apparent; dependent on

Indeed, many biologists claim that this attribute, the ability to (iii)__________, or, to put it more sharply, to make individuals subordinate their self-interest to the needs of the group, lies at the root of human achievement.

The prioritization by academic institutions of scholarly publication as a criterion for advancement in science departments discourages researchers from sharing their data with peers. As a result, scientists operate in a realm where the incentive structure serves as an (i) __________ greater (ii) __________ in the profession.The prioritization by academic institutions of scholarly publication as a criterion for advancement in science departments discourages researchers from sharing their data with peers. As a result, scientists operate in a realm where the incentive structure serves as an (i) __________ greater (ii) __________ in the profession.
Blank 1

Carving materials such as stone, bone, antler, wood, and ivory were (iii) __________, but paper and drawing tools were unknown until introduced by early explorers and missionaries.Inuit print making is less (i) __________ than carving in that it does not have substantial historical precedents, although there are (ii) __________ incised carvings on bone or antler, facial tattoo marks or inlay skin work on clothing, mitts and footwear. Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, G　traditional; affinities with; available locally

答案：B, C　plethora; surfeit

Blank 1
Blank 2

Not only was Pushkin's personal correspondence intercepted and his movements (iii)__________, but Tsar Nicholas I's decision to oversee Pushkin's career obliged Pushkin to submit all his manuscripts for inspection.Even the reader acquainted with the outlines of Pushkin's biography will be (i)__________ the (ii)__________ so vividly conveyed in Binyon's biography. Not only was Pushkin's personal correspondence intercepted and his movements (iii)__________, but Tsar Nicholas I's decision to oversee Pushkin's career obliged Pushkin to submit all his manuscripts for inspection.

答案：A, C　nuanced; complex

#441 TC DOUBLE
Scientists now recognize that the human brain has remarkable plasticity and potential for restoring lost function. Following injuries to the brain that cause permanent but localized damage to brain tissue, neurological deficits can often be (i) __________ by special training. In other words, changes in the brain can be (ii) __________.Scientists now recognize that the human brain has remarkable plasticity and potential for restoring lost function. Following injuries to the brain that cause permanent but localized damage to brain tissue, neurological deficits can often be (i) __________ by special training. In other words, changes in the brain can be (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, D　ameliorated; electively produced

Thus, when we are considering a role for sleep in human memory consolidation, we are referring to a possible role in the (ii)__________ encoding of information and optimizing of recall, not a (iii)__________ of sleep for recalling events of the prior day.It is possible for humans to go 40 or more hours without sleep and still be able to (i)__________ information acquired at the beginning of the sleepless period. Thus, when we are considering a role for sleep in human memory consolidation, we are referring to a possible role in the (ii)__________ encoding of information and optimizing of recall, not a (iii)__________ of sleep for recalling events of the prior day.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, G　disgorge; longer-term; requirement

答案：B, C　incapable of; hopeless at

答案：B, E　ancillary to; subsidiary to

Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：C, E　emulate; anachronistic

#448 TC TRIPLE
To pay for the extra spending under this international poverty plan, each American would have to contribute less than the cost of buying a premium cup of coffee once a week. But financial aid is not (i)__________, and even if the funding recommended here were to (ii)__________, the grandest objectives may well remain unfulfilled. Nonetheless, carefully targeted aid can reward responsible governments, (iii)__________ individual initiative, and alleviate suffering. Many will think that's worth a cup of coffee.To pay for the extra spending under this international poverty plan, each American would have to contribute less than the cost of buying a premium cup of coffee once a week. But financial aid is not (i)__________, and even if the funding recommended here were to (ii)__________, the grandest objectives may well remain unfulfilled. Nonetheless, carefully targeted aid can reward responsible governments, (iii)__________ individual initiative, and alleviate suffering. Many will think that's worth a cup of coffee.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, I　a panacea; materialize; encourage
malady
malady: standard unit headword
#449 SE
We might be much closer to building the next generation of observatories if competing groups of astronomers had not __________ repeated chances to collaborate; instead, three separate projects with common goals are fighting one another for funding.We might be much closer to building the next generation of observatories if competing groups of astronomers had not __________ repeated chances to collaborate; instead, three separate projects with common goals are fighting one another for funding.
答案：A, D　spurned; rebuffed
sabotage
sabotage: standard unit headword
#450 TC DOUBLE
Britain's deteriorating economy after 1945 was (i) __________ by politicians who favored the manufacturing sector over the service sector: rather than attempting to (ii) __________ the decline of manufacturing, they should have promoted service industries.Britain's deteriorating economy after 1945 was (i) __________ by politicians who favored the manufacturing sector over the service sector: rather than attempting to (ii) __________ the decline of manufacturing, they should have promoted service industries.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, E　mishandled; arrest
augmentrather
augment: standard unit headword | rather: standard unit headword
#451 TC TRIPLE
The motives of many major investors in Pop Art have arguably been to a large extent, (i)__________. These collectors demonstrate and enhance their power over the art market by establishing seemingly arbitrary works of art as priceless. This phenomenon reveals that (ii)__________ is not (iii)__________ of truth or beauty, but simply a trick of investment capital.The motives of many major investors in Pop Art have arguably been to a large extent, (i)__________. These collectors demonstrate and enhance their power over the art market by establishing seemingly arbitrary works of art as priceless. This phenomenon reveals that (ii)__________ is not (iii)__________ of truth or beauty, but simply a trick of investment capital.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, G　self-aggrandizing; value; a product
virtuosity
virtuosity: standard unit headword
#452 SE
The author's point is not that people should be __________ science; he is careful to point out that what science is discovering about humans, for example, is certainly important and worthy of careful consideration.The author's point is not that people should be __________ science; he is careful to point out that what science is discovering about humans, for example, is certainly important and worthy of careful consideration.
答案：B, E　dismissive of; scornful of
zealous
zealous: standard unit headword
#453 TC DOUBLE
Sinden notes that in the face of warnings about the perils of climate change, the need to agree on strategies for reducing greenhouse-gas emissions is increasingly urgent. The question of who should reduce emissions is not (i) __________. Rather, according to Sinden, it is the question of who should bear the costs of these reductions that is (ii) __________.Sinden notes that in the face of warnings about the perils of climate change, the need to agree on strategies for reducing greenhouse-gas emissions is increasingly urgent. The question of who should reduce emissions is not (i) __________. Rather, according to Sinden, it is the question of who should bear the costs of these reductions that is (ii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：A, E　particularly vexed; contentious
prescientrather
prescient: standard unit headword | rather: standard unit headword
#454 TC TRIPLE
Human-caused disturbances, such as habitat destruction and the introduction of nonnative species, are among the leading causes of plant and animal population declines. Most populations are affected by a combination of adverse human pressures, each of which is in itself insufficient to (i)__________ a population crash. Therefore, studies of population declines that (ii)__________ individual factors and thus (iii)__________ potential interactions may lead to improper management of declining species.Human-caused disturbances, such as habitat destruction and the introduction of nonnative species, are among the leading causes of plant and animal population declines. Most populations are affected by a combination of adverse human pressures, each of which is in itself insufficient to (i)__________ a population crash. Therefore, studies of population declines that (ii)__________ individual factors and thus (iii)__________ potential interactions may lead to improper management of declining species.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, E, H　trigger; focus on; overlook
anticipate
anticipate: standard unit headword
#455 SE
Concerned to upend the standard top-down approach to game design, Flanagan calls on game designers to __________ the typical model with one that is less hierarchical.Concerned to upend the standard top-down approach to game design, Flanagan calls on game designers to __________ the typical model with one that is less hierarchical.
答案：B, F　supplant; replace
augment
augment: standard unit headword
#456 TC DOUBLE
Wald's decision to tell Rosetta Tharpe's story to a wider audience in Shout, Sister, Shout! (i) __________ the rigor of the book's scholarship or the acuity of its theory. The scholarly quality of the work is (ii) __________ on every page, and yet it is not a dry, specialist academic tome.Wald's decision to tell Rosetta Tharpe's story to a wider audience in Shout, Sister, Shout! (i) __________ the rigor of the book's scholarship or the acuity of its theory. The scholarly quality of the work is (ii) __________ on every page, and yet it is not a dry, specialist academic tome.
Blank 1
Blank 2
答案：B, D　does not compromise; evident

reflect: standard unit headword | rigor: standard unit headword
#457 TC TRIPLE
For many years Cole experienced (i)__________ his professional circle. He was a (ii)__________ figure in the Middle East Studies Association of North America, editing for five years its flagship publication, and in 2004 he was even elected the association's incoming president. But because his research focused on highly (iii)__________ aspects of the eighteenth-and-nineteenth century Middle East, he was unlikely to achieve any sort of public acclaim.For many years Cole experienced (i)__________ his professional circle. He was a (ii)__________ figure in the Middle East Studies Association of North America, editing for five years its flagship publication, and in 2004 he was even elected the association's incoming president. But because his research focused on highly (iii)__________ aspects of the eighteenth-and-nineteenth century Middle East, he was unlikely to achieve any sort of public acclaim.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, H　anonymity outside; leading; esoteric
provocative
provocative: standard unit headword
#458 SE
During winter, the nearshore waters in the northwestern Gulf of Mexico can become quite __________: storms carry silt in from land and stir up the mud of the seafloor.During winter, the nearshore waters in the northwestern Gulf of Mexico can become quite __________: storms carry silt in from land and stir up the mud of the seafloor.
答案：B, F　turbid; roiled
stratify
stratify: standard unit headword
#459 TC TRIPLE
The essential difference between writing nonfiction and fiction is that the artist can (i)__________ a completed vision of the world, while the journalist never can, the real world being always (ii)__________. Art provides freedom from the bewildering complexities of constant change. Indeed, it is the very (iii)__________ of well-wrought fiction that can sometimes make it feel more real than reality.The essential difference between writing nonfiction and fiction is that the artist can (i)__________ a completed vision of the world, while the journalist never can, the real world being always (ii)__________. Art provides freedom from the bewildering complexities of constant change. Indeed, it is the very (iii)__________ of well-wrought fiction that can sometimes make it feel more real than reality.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, I　realize; unfinished; clarity
shun
shun: standard unit headword
#460 SE
The artist reworked certain of his etchings in order to make the scenes they depict more logical, yet, paradoxically, the pictorial space seems to become more __________ the more realistically it is defined.The artist reworked certain of his etchings in order to make the scenes they depict more logical, yet, paradoxically, the pictorial space seems to become more __________ the more realistically it is defined.
答案：D, F　fanciful; chimerical

cogent: standard unit headword
#461 TC TRIPLE
Dirac may have (i)__________ the company of other theoretical physicists, but he often (ii)__________, rarely (iii)__________ group discussions and almost never collaborating with others in his own work.Dirac may have (i)__________ the company of other theoretical physicists, but he often (ii)__________, rarely (iii)__________ group discussions and almost never collaborating with others in his own work.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, H　appreciated; kept them at a distance; contributing to

ponder: standard unit headword

The author remains keen to settle old scores, passing up few opportunities to __________ former colleagues.The author remains keen to settle old scores, passing up few opportunities to __________ former colleagues.
答案：A, C　slam; vilify

shun: standard unit headword
#463 TC TRIPLE
In its earliest days, the telegraph inspired (i)__________ without precedent in the annals of technology. A new sense of (ii)__________ came across: a sense that the world was in (iii)__________, that life for one's children and grandchildren would be very different, all because of this wondrous force and its uses.In its earliest days, the telegraph inspired (i)__________ without precedent in the annals of technology. A new sense of (ii)__________ came across: a sense that the world was in (iii)__________, that life for one's children and grandchildren would be very different, all because of this wondrous force and its uses.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, G　exhilaration; futurity; a state of change

Although the philosopher frequently dealt with __________ theoretical topics, she was capable of writing in a style that was comprehensible to a general audience.Although the philosopher frequently dealt with __________ theoretical topics, she was capable of writing in a style that was comprehensible to a general audience.
答案：B, E　abstruse; recondite

Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, H　tendentious; selective; omitting

Offscreen, actor Madhur Jaffrey is the author of some thirteen books about the cuisine of her native India; therefore, it is not __________ that Indian food was important in her motion picture Chutney Popcorn.Offscreen, actor Madhur Jaffrey is the author of some thirteen books about the cuisine of her native India; therefore, it is not __________ that Indian food was important in her motion picture Chutney Popcorn.
答案：A, E　happenstance; incidental

In the nineteenth century the (i)__________ advanced mechanical printing techniques made it possible for newspaper owners to print newspapers cheaply and in mass quantities, but unlike many other mechanized industries, where machines (ii)__________ workers, the new printing machines required trained compositors to run them, thereby (iii)__________ the demand for skilled printing labor.In the nineteenth century the (i)__________ advanced mechanical printing techniques made it possible for newspaper owners to print newspapers cheaply and in mass quantities, but unlike many other mechanized industries, where machines (ii)__________ workers, the new printing machines required trained compositors to run them, thereby (iii)__________ the demand for skilled printing labor.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, I　wide application of; marginalized; increasing

答案：C, D　inaugurated; engendered

represent: standard unit headword
#469 TC TRIPLE
In the early twentieth century, rural women in the United States could feel caught between, on one hand, national myths upholding a "natural" lifestyle that by definition seemed to eschew science and technology, and, on the other hand, a desire to (i) __________ the new, modern standards of mechanized household practice that (ii) __________ their (iii) __________ by aligning them more and more with city women and modern life generally.In the early twentieth century, rural women in the United States could feel caught between, on one hand, national myths upholding a "natural" lifestyle that by definition seemed to eschew science and technology, and, on the other hand, a desire to (i) __________ the new, modern standards of mechanized household practice that (ii) __________ their (iii) __________ by aligning them more and more with city women and modern life generally.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, G　adopt; threatened to compromise; distinctiveness
accentuate
accentuate: standard unit headword

答案：A, C　senescence; decrepitude

Studies of conservation attitudes of people in South Africa and Tanzania have found a positive correlation between affluence and conservation attitudes, but it is unlikely that an improvement in the living standards of communities near protected areas will inevitably (iii) __________ the long-term viability of many species within the protected areas.

Blank 2
Blank 3

Any of those behaviors might also help explain why these people have lower disease rates.In general, observational studies tend to (i) __________ the effect of diet on overall health because a healthy diet is often (ii) __________ a healthy lifestyle. People who watch what they eat probably exercise more, avoid smoking, and follow other behaviors (iii) __________ good health. Blank 3

#477 TC TRIPLE
Books that diagnose problems in democratic governance usually conclude with suggestions for procedural change. By contrast, the authors of this new study have, in their previous studies, always stuck closely to the data in their work, (i) __________ to propose measures that might (ii) __________ the circumstances they document. In this new study, however, in a break from their usual (iii) __________, they conclude by outlining some possible reforms.Books that diagnose problems in democratic governance usually conclude with suggestions for procedural change. By contrast, the authors of this new study have, in their previous studies, always stuck closely to the data in their work, (i) __________ to propose measures that might (ii) __________ the circumstances they document. In this new study, however, in a break from their usual (iii) __________, they conclude by outlining some possible reforms.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, E, G　forgoing opportunities; ameliorate; reticence

anticipate: standard unit headword
#478 SE
In principle, the idea of an ice age is __________: in the past, it was colder, glaciers were much more extensive than they are today, and huge ice sheets covered large sections of continents that are now ice-free.In principle, the idea of an ice age is __________: in the past, it was colder, glaciers were much more extensive than they are today, and huge ice sheets covered large sections of continents that are now ice-free.
答案：C, D　simple; straightforward

One of the problems with recent books that perpetuate the view of Jane Austen as moral tutor, a sort of Miss Manners for the ages, is that they understand manners as (i)__________, as near-universal and timeless behavioral ideals or, worse still, a set of rules to be followed. It is crucial, however, to (ii)__________. In other words, one needs to resist (iii)__________ generic "manners" with more linguistically and historically precise designations, not least because Austen herself distinguishes.One of the problems with recent books that perpetuate the view of Jane Austen as moral tutor, a sort of Miss Manners for the ages, is that they understand manners as (i)__________, as near-universal and timeless behavioral ideals or, worse still, a set of rules to be followed. It is crucial, however, to (ii)__________. In other words, one needs to resist (iii)__________ generic "manners" with more linguistically and historically precise designations, not least because Austen herself distinguishes.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, E, H　monolithic; differentiate various behaviors; conflating

#480 TC TRIPLE
If companies were taxed based on the amount of their atmospheric emissions of pollutants, they could make their own decisions about how to alter their operations. Firms could compare the cost of paying taxes with the cost of preventing the emissions in the first place and would, in most cases, do a bit of both. Firms that can reduce pollution (i)__________ would reduce it quite (ii)__________, while those that can only do so at great cost would (iii)__________.If companies were taxed based on the amount of their atmospheric emissions of pollutants, they could make their own decisions about how to alter their operations. Firms could compare the cost of paying taxes with the cost of preventing the emissions in the first place and would, in most cases, do a bit of both. Firms that can reduce pollution (i)__________ would reduce it quite (ii)__________, while those that can only do so at great cost would (iii)__________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, F, G　cheaply; substantially; curtail less

anticipate: standard unit headword

The fantastic conceit of the film's plot is that global warming (i)__________ global freezing. After a chunk of Antarctic ice the size of Rhode Island suddenly melts, most of what follows--an instant ice age, for example--is impossible as science but not as metaphor. The data from the Greenland ice sheet show that, over the past hundred thousand years, temperatures have often (ii)__________--so often that it is our own relatively static experience of climate that should now be seen as (iii)__________.The fantastic conceit of the film's plot is that global warming (i)__________ global freezing. After a chunk of Antarctic ice the size of Rhode Island suddenly melts, most of what follows--an instant ice age, for example--is impossible as science but not as metaphor. The data from the Greenland ice sheet show that, over the past hundred thousand years, temperatures have often (ii)__________--so often that it is our own relatively static experience of climate that should now be seen as (iii)__________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, H　produces; swung wildly; exceptional

authentic: standard unit headword
#482 TC TRIPLE
Describing cosmopolitanism and provincialism as (i) __________ concepts misses part of their relationship. They are actually (ii) __________, both in the strictly semiotic sense that the idea of cosmopolitanism is (iii) __________ unless we can oppose it to something like provincialism and in a more practical sense: much of cosmopolitanism involves recombining elements of provincial cultures.Describing cosmopolitanism and provincialism as (i) __________ concepts misses part of their relationship. They are actually (ii) __________, both in the strictly semiotic sense that the idea of cosmopolitanism is (iii) __________ unless we can oppose it to something like provincialism and in a more practical sense: much of cosmopolitanism involves recombining elements of provincial cultures.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, G　antithetical; mutually constitutive; meaningless

No matter how much input orchestra players are allowed in choosing a music director, they never feel they have enough. Regardless, their criticisms of Webster's expertise and stature within the field are nothing short of (iii) __________. Would they really prefer some of the other potential candidates whose names have been released?It's possible that the (i) __________ of the musicians about the director's appointment is mostly an expression of (ii) __________ the selection process. No matter how much input orchestra players are allowed in choosing a music director, they never feel they have enough. Regardless, their criticisms of Webster's expertise and stature within the field are nothing short of (iii) __________. Would they really prefer some of the other potential candidates whose names have been released?

Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, F, I　carping; disgruntlement regarding; preposterous

prescient: standard unit headword
#484 TC TRIPLE
Singers of the tropicalist movement in Brazil goaded the government by purposefully invoking stereotypical images of Brazil as a tropical paradise only to (i) __________ them with pointed references to political and social problems. But (ii) __________ in giving offense, the tropicalist singers also managed to annoy the Brazilian left, which shared their social concerns. They embraced an irreverent politics and (iii) __________, epitomized by the coexistence of bossa nova and rock in the same song, that ignored certain leftist calls for folkloric authenticity.Singers of the tropicalist movement in Brazil goaded the government by purposefully invoking stereotypical images of Brazil as a tropical paradise only to (i) __________ them with pointed references to political and social problems. But (ii) __________ in giving offense, the tropicalist singers also managed to annoy the Brazilian left, which shared their social concerns. They embraced an irreverent politics and (iii) __________, epitomized by the coexistence of bossa nova and rock in the same song, that ignored certain leftist calls for folkloric authenticity.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, H　subvert; ecumenical; a pastiche of styles

virtuosity: standard unit headword

Blank 2
Blank 3

Nineteenth-century England presents something of (i) __________, at least to those who believe that (ii) __________ best promotes business ethics. The defining characteristic of English company law at that time was its extreme degree of permissiveness: laissez-faire ruled in Parliament, caveat emptor in the courts, creating the ideal breeding ground for fraud and dishonesty. Yet these were the years when London became the financial center of the world and established its status as a (iii) __________ place to do business.Nineteenth-century England presents something of (i) __________, at least to those who believe that (ii) __________ best promotes business ethics. The defining characteristic of English company law at that time was its extreme degree of permissiveness: laissez-faire ruled in Parliament, caveat emptor in the courts, creating the ideal breeding ground for fraud and dishonesty. Yet these were the years when London became the financial center of the world and established its status as a (iii) __________ place to do business.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, H　an enigma; regulation; comparatively safe

(i) __________ dams that have outlived their usefulness--or whose social and economic utility is (ii) __________ by the environmental harm they do--is an idea that is catching on. Over the past 6 years, some 175 dams have been dismantled across the United States, and more than 600 over the past century. That's just a drop in the millpond, however, given that there are perhaps 2.5 million dams in the United States and that most of those removed to date have been relatively (iii) __________.(i) __________ dams that have outlived their usefulness--or whose social and economic utility is (ii) __________ by the environmental harm they do--is an idea that is catching on. Over the past 6 years, some 175 dams have been dismantled across the United States, and more than 600 over the past century. That's just a drop in the millpond, however, given that there are perhaps 2.5 million dams in the United States and that most of those removed to date have been relatively (iii) __________.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, E, H　Demolishing; overshadowed; insignificant

In other words, nascent warrior culture and identity was (iii) __________ the noble culture of Kyoto.As Japanese warriors took up permanent residence in the city of Kyoto during the Muromachi period (1338-1573 C.E.), they (i) __________ that city's aristocratic culture, using similar principles when creating their own clan rules and forming a parallel set of warrior etiquette that (ii) __________ aristocratic traditions. Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, E, G　adopted; mimicked; rooted in
