Smith's poetry that (i) __________ its formal and aesthetic qualities are (ii) __________, as Smith's formal choices are ultimately (iii) __________ her political arguments.Analyses of Tracy K. Smith's poetry that (i) __________ its formal and aesthetic qualities are (ii) __________, as Smith's formal choices are ultimately (iii) __________ her political arguments.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, G　prioritize; deficient; ancillary to
commend
commend: standard unit headword
#491 TC TRIPLE
The purpose of the Endangered Species Act, which was signed into law in 1973, is to conserve species at risk of extinction and the ecosystems upon which these species depend. (i) __________ a requirement simply to prevent extinction, the Act entails an (ii) __________ mandate: to "use ... all methods and procedures which are necessary to bring any [listed] species ... to the point at which the measures provided pursuant to this [Act] are no longer necessary." According to the Act, in other words, conservation requires the (iii) __________ of listed species.The purpose of the Endangered Species Act, which was signed into law in 1973, is to conserve species at risk of extinction and the ecosystems upon which these species depend. (i) __________ a requirement simply to prevent extinction, the Act entails an (ii) __________ mandate: to "use ... all methods and procedures which are necessary to bring any [listed] species ... to the point at which the measures provided pursuant to this [Act] are no longer necessary." According to the Act, in other words, conservation requires the (iii) __________ of listed species.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, F, H　More than; affirmative; recovery
hamper
hamper: standard unit headword
#492 TC TRIPLE
For scientists who wish to conduct research in the national parks, the new plan promises a warm welcome in place of the (i) __________, or even resistance, that often greets requests for research permits. The plan says that "parks can and should be centers for broad scientific research and inquiry." It also maintains that scientific research should be (ii) __________ in parks where it can be performed without (iii) __________ other park values.For scientists who wish to conduct research in the national parks, the new plan promises a warm welcome in place of the (i) __________, or even resistance, that often greets requests for research permits. The plan says that "parks can and should be centers for broad scientific research and inquiry." It also maintains that scientific research should be (ii) __________ in parks where it can be performed without (iii) __________ other park values.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, F, H　indifference; facilitated; jeopardizing

augment: standard unit headword

The gravity of the issues discussed requires that we (ii) __________ such (iii) __________: we need publications that engage the broader public in discussions of how we interact with the natural world.

#494 TC TRIPLE
History is about recognizing continuities and therefore the historian must (i) __________ stories that rope together the disparate worlds of past and present. But it is also true that honest history arises from a sense of (ii) __________. It is the experience of discontinuity that alerts us to the (iii) __________ of the past and sets us looking for narratives to capture that unfamiliar entity.History is about recognizing continuities and therefore the historian must (i) __________ stories that rope together the disparate worlds of past and present. But it is also true that honest history arises from a sense of (ii) __________. It is the experience of discontinuity that alerts us to the (iii) __________ of the past and sets us looking for narratives to capture that unfamiliar entity.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, H　weave; interruptedness; strangeness

#495 TC TRIPLE
Taruskin's history of Western music, (i) __________ its remarkable length, makes no claims to comprehensiveness. Taruskin is punctilious, and justifiably proud, in emphasizing that his book is a work of history, not a survey of the repertory. In fact, he (ii) __________ criticism on grounds of omission by cheerfully admitting he has (iii) __________ such side-branch composers as Vaughan Williams, who maintained the British symphonic tradition into the mid-twentieth century.Taruskin's history of Western music, (i) __________ its remarkable length, makes no claims to comprehensiveness. Taruskin is punctilious, and justifiably proud, in emphasizing that his book is a work of history, not a survey of the repertory. In fact, he (ii) __________ criticism on grounds of omission by cheerfully admitting he has (iii) __________ such side-branch composers as Vaughan Williams, who maintained the British symphonic tradition into the mid-twentieth century.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, D, I　despite; disarms; ignored
reflect
reflect: standard unit headword
#496 TC TRIPLE
The notion of popular science or of the public understanding of science is very much a concern in today's world, where we can see (i) __________ between science and nonscience. But in the nineteenth century, there was still an enviable mixing and cross-fertilization between seemingly (ii) __________ subjects, and there was no (iii) __________ what counted as science, let alone how a public understanding might differ from any other sort of understanding.The notion of popular science or of the public understanding of science is very much a concern in today's world, where we can see (i) __________ between science and nonscience. But in the nineteenth century, there was still an enviable mixing and cross-fertilization between seemingly (ii) __________ subjects, and there was no (iii) __________ what counted as science, let alone how a public understanding might differ from any other sort of understanding.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, I　a clear demarcation; disparate; consensus on

permeable: standard unit headword
#497 TC TRIPLE
Every few years, as a reviewer, one encounters a novel whose (i) __________ are so many in number, and so (ii) __________, that to correct them fully would produce a text that exceeded the novel itself in length. Faced with such a book, one wishes only to let it slip quietly to the seabed of culture, there to join thousands of other (iii) __________ books in their slow disintegration.Every few years, as a reviewer, one encounters a novel whose (i) __________ are so many in number, and so (ii) __________, that to correct them fully would produce a text that exceeded the novel itself in length. Faced with such a book, one wishes only to let it slip quietly to the seabed of culture, there to join thousands of other (iii) __________ books in their slow disintegration.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, D, H　ineptitudes; thoroughgoing; incompetent
indispensable
indispensable: standard unit headword
#498 TC TRIPLE
Climbing mountains in search of an awe-inspiring view can be (i) __________ experience. Too often, the peak seems forever out of reach, always disappearing over the next crest. It's a feeling that (ii) __________ physicists, who have spent much of the last century trudging up a seemingly endless slope in search of truly cosmic vistas. It is proving (iii) __________ climb.Climbing mountains in search of an awe-inspiring view can be (i) __________ experience. Too often, the peak seems forever out of reach, always disappearing over the next crest. It's a feeling that (ii) __________ physicists, who have spent much of the last century trudging up a seemingly endless slope in search of truly cosmic vistas. It is proving (iii) __________ climb.

Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, E, G　a disappointing; must be shared by; a difficult

#499 TC TRIPLE
In 1859 the editor of one newspaper in the western United States lamented that "the popular idea of Western Literature" consisted of such cliched topics as "log cabins, rough speech, dare-devil boldness, bear-hunting and cornhusking." With Easterners still (i) __________ the representation of the West, western writers had to publish in the East in order to succeed, despite a (ii) __________ literary scene in many western cities. Not surprisingly, this editor urged Westerners to (iii) __________ representations of the West created by Easterners.In 1859 the editor of one newspaper in the western United States lamented that "the popular idea of Western Literature" consisted of such cliched topics as "log cabins, rough speech, dare-devil boldness, bear-hunting and cornhusking." With Easterners still (i) __________ the representation of the West, western writers had to publish in the East in order to succeed, despite a (ii) __________ literary scene in many western cities. Not surprisingly, this editor urged Westerners to (iii) __________ representations of the West created by Easterners.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, H　controlling; burgeoning; reject

cherish: standard unit headword

However, evidence of significant contact between the ancient Mediterranean and sub-Saharan Africa has been (ii) __________, rendering these exogenous explanations (iii) __________.

Blank 3
答案：A, D, I　recipients of; meager; tenuous

#501 TC TRIPLE
This is not the first time science has been invoked to argue for inherent limits to the capabilities of technology. The most (i) __________ of such predictions made in the name of science, such as that we would never go to the Moon, have underestimated the (ii) __________ of engineers. But what these blunders had in common was that no law of physics, or at least no (iii) __________ one, was invoked to support the claim.This is not the first time science has been invoked to argue for inherent limits to the capabilities of technology. The most (i) __________ of such predictions made in the name of science, such as that we would never go to the Moon, have underestimated the (ii) __________ of engineers. But what these blunders had in common was that no law of physics, or at least no (iii) __________ one, was invoked to support the claim.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, F, H　embarrassing; ingenuity; established

Wolosky claims that Ella Wheeler Wilcox joined other women poets such as Julia Ward Howe, Frances Harper, and Charlotte Perkins Gilman in critiquing materialism and possessive individualism. Wolosky’s description (i)__________ the poetry of Gilman, Howe, and Harper, but it is not entirely (ii)__________ in the case of Wilcox, who hardly (iii)__________ the materialism of her time. Rather, Wilcox seems to have embraced the amassing of private property.Wolosky claims that Ella Wheeler Wilcox joined other women poets such as Julia Ward Howe, Frances Harper, and Charlotte Perkins Gilman in critiquing materialism and possessive individualism. Wolosky’s description (i)__________ the poetry of Gilman, Howe, and Harper, but it is not entirely (ii)__________ in the case of Wilcox, who hardly (iii)__________ the materialism of her time. Rather, Wilcox seems to have embraced the amassing of private property.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：A, D, I　fits; apt; impugned

Recent proposals for fixing the climate have taken the form of large-scale geoengineering projects such as launching mirrors into space to reflect solar radiation away from Earth, undertakings that are vastly more (i)__________ than anything a nineteenth-century rainmaker could have cooked up. What is unclear, as one looks back at the history of weather modification research, is whether this resourceful ambition will be (ii)__________, or if, by contrast, it serves to make the scientific community's (iii)__________ that much more devastating.Recent proposals for fixing the climate have taken the form of large-scale geoengineering projects such as launching mirrors into space to reflect solar radiation away from Earth, undertakings that are vastly more (i)__________ than anything a nineteenth-century rainmaker could have cooked up. What is unclear, as one looks back at the history of weather modification research, is whether this resourceful ambition will be (ii)__________, or if, by contrast, it serves to make the scientific community's (iii)__________ that much more devastating.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, F, H　enterprising; productive; impotence

(i)__________ was, in a way, his very theme. Was ever a bookish soul so cracklingly unmediated, so (ii)__________ raw life? 答案：A, E, I　energy; flush with; tedium

The author is adept at dealing with trivial details in the poets he studies, but he is quite (ii) __________ when it comes to illuminating the overall vision. The verbal intricacy and panache of his writing contrast with its conceptual (iii) __________. If there are very few authentic ideas in the poems under consideration, there is also a shortage of them in the author's commentary.Perhaps the best one can say of this new book of criticism is that it is scintillatingly (i) __________. The author is adept at dealing with trivial details in the poets he studies, but he is quite (ii) __________ when it comes to illuminating the overall vision. If there are very few authentic ideas in the poems under consideration, there is also a shortage of them in the author's commentary.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：C, D, I　shallow; indifferent; poverty

Native American art is not a (i) __________ enterprise: it reflects differences among artists' personal, community, and tribal experience. As Native American artists continue to create the art that is valid for them, however, the (ii) __________ in Native American art between traditional and modern styles (primarily promulgated by non-Native American critics) has become outmoded. There is heightened recognition that placing Native American art in these two opposing categories has had a (iii) __________ effect on both artists and viewers.Native American art is not a (i) __________ enterprise: it reflects differences among artists' personal, community, and tribal experience. As Native American artists continue to create the art that is valid for them, however, the (ii) __________ in Native American art between traditional and modern styles (primarily promulgated by non-Native American critics) has become outmoded. There is heightened recognition that placing Native American art in these two opposing categories has had a (iii) __________ effect on both artists and viewers.
Blank 1
Blank 2
Blank 3
答案：B, D, H　monolithic; supposed distinction; deleterious
